semgrep_note: Semgrep Pro engine enabled for all scans; no authentication error encountered. Standard rulesets (golang, security-audit, trail-of-bits) produced 0 findings on targeted paths, reflecting Grafana's mature security posture for common patterns. All findings are from custom rules targeting KB-specific blind spots.

findings.description: mysql.NewContext() called without mysql.WithDisableFileWrites(true). The IsReadOnly engine flag set on sqle.New() is bypassed because plan.Into.IsReadOnly() delegates to the child SELECT node (returns true). Combined with *sqlparser.Into on the allowlist (parser_allow.go:113), an authenticated user with sqlExpressions feature enabled can execute SELECT ... INTO OUTFILE to write arbitrary files on the Grafana server process.
findings.attack_vector: POST /api/ds/query with type=sql and expression='SELECT 1 INTO OUTFILE /path/to/file'
preconditions: sqlExpressions feature flag enabled by admin
findings.triage_note: Confirmed. Both mechanisms independently insufficient: (1) IsReadOnly bypass via INTO.IsReadOnly()->SELECT.IsReadOnly()->true, (2) DisableFileWrites not called. Fix requires removing *sqlparser.Into from allowlist AND calling WithDisableFileWrites(true).
findings.remediation: Remove *sqlparser.Into from parser_allow.go allowlist. Add mysql.WithDisableFileWrites(true) option to mysql.NewContext call at db.go:71.

findings.description: *sqlparser.Into is explicitly whitelisted in the SQL expression engine AST allowlist. This allows SELECT ... INTO OUTFILE and SELECT ... INTO DUMPFILE statements to pass AST validation and reach the go-mysql-server execution engine.
findings.attack_vector: SQL expression with INTO OUTFILE clause passes AllowQuery() check
preconditions: sqlExpressions feature flag enabled by admin
findings.triage_note: Confirmed root cause. *sqlparser.Into on allowlist is intentionally defensive (comment says the allowlist controls what's allowed) but *sqlparser.Into should not be in the allowlist given the file write risk.
findings.remediation: Remove the *sqlparser.Into case from allowedNode() in parser_allow.go.

findings.description: /avatar/:hash route uses reqSignedIn middleware. When [auth.anonymous] enabled=true, AllowAnonymous=true on context causes requireLogin=false (auth.go:216), allowing unauthenticated Gravatar proxy requests. Each cache miss triggers outbound HTTP to secure.gravatar.com. With concurrent unique hashes and a 2000-entry LRU, goroutine exhaustion and memory pressure DoS is possible.
findings.attack_vector: GET /avatar/<unique_hash> from unauthenticated client when anonymous auth enabled
findings.triage_note: Confirmed. Fix should use reqSignedInNoAnonymous (sets ReqNoAnonymous=true) to block anonymous access to this endpoint specifically.
findings.remediation: Change reqSignedIn to reqSignedInNoAnonymous on the /avatar/:hash route at api.go:605.

findings.description: K8s API snapshot delete-by-deleteKey handler performs RBAC check (ActionSnapshotsDelete) but does not verify snapshot.OrgID matches the requester's org before calling DeleteWithKey(). The legacy REST API correctly checks OrgID mismatch. An authenticated user in any org with ActionSnapshotsDelete can delete snapshots from other organizations using a leaked deleteKey.
findings.attack_vector: DELETE /apis/dashboard.grafana.app/.../snapshots/delete/:deleteKey with cross-org deleteKey
preconditions: Knowledge of deleteKey from another org (log leak, shared URL, API response)
findings.triage_note: Confirmed. CVE-2024-1313 fix was applied to REST API path but not ported to the K8s API path in routes.go.
findings.remediation: Add org isolation check in routes.go after fetching snapshot: verify snapshot.OrgID matches requester's org before calling DeleteWithKey.

findings.description: CancelSnapshot service interface signature lacks orgID parameter. The implementation (cloudmigration.go:796) calls UpdateSnapshot which executes SQL UPDATE cloud_migration_snapshot SET status=? WHERE session_uid=? AND uid=? without org_id constraint. An admin in org-B can cancel an in-progress snapshot owned by org-A if they obtain the session_uid and snapshot_uid values.
findings.attack_vector: POST /api/cloudmigration/migration/:uid/snapshot/:snapshotUid/cancel with cross-org session_uid/snapshot_uid
preconditions: Knowledge of target org's session_uid and snapshot_uid
findings.triage_note: Confirmed. CancelSnapshot interface has no orgID. UpdateSnapshot SQL has no org_id WHERE constraint (xorm_store.go:228).
findings.remediation: Add orgID parameter to CancelSnapshot interface and implementation. Add org_id to UpdateSnapshot SQL WHERE clauses.

findings.description: http.ServeFile(c.Resp, c.Req, result.FilePath) called without verifying result.FilePath is within an expected temp directory. result.FilePath comes from the rendering service which is a separate process communicating via HTTP. If the renderer JWT is forged (default token '-'), an attacker could manipulate the rendering response to return an arbitrary FilePath, causing ServeFile to expose arbitrary files.
findings.attack_vector: Forge renderer JWT with default token '-', submit render request, return manipulated FilePath in response
findings.triage_note: Confirmed. No directory confinement check before ServeFile. Chained with SAST-003 or H2 (JWT forgery) for full exploit.
findings.remediation: Add strings.HasPrefix(filepath.Clean(result.FilePath), expectedTempDir) check before http.ServeFile call.

findings.description: CodeQL request-forgery: HTTP request URL depends on user-provided value from GMS API response (presigned upload URL). Grafana performs HTTP PUT to GMS-provided presigned URL without destination validation. A compromised GMS can supply an internal network URL (169.254.169.254, localhost, VPC CIDR) to exfiltrate migration snapshot data.
findings.attack_vector: Compromised GMS returns presigned URL pointing to internal network endpoint
findings.triage_note: Confirmed SSRF vector. CodeQL independently confirms H4 finding from prior audit phase.
findings.remediation: Validate presigned URL against allowlist of expected storage domains (e.g., amazonaws.com, blob.core.windows.net). Block RFC1918 and link-local destinations.

findings.description: CodeQL flags symlink extraction at line 99 in plugin zip extraction. There is a validation check via isSymlinkRelativeTo() at line 153, but CodeQL's unsafe-unzip-symlink query fires because the dstPath is used as symlink target without resolving intermediate symlinks in the directory chain. A multi-level symlink chain in plugin archive could traverse outside plugin directory.
findings.attack_vector: Plugin zip with crafted symlink chain where intermediate directories are themselves symlinks
findings.triage_note: Medium confidence. isSymlinkRelativeTo() provides path-based check but does not resolve intermediate symlinks. The lgtm[go/zipslip] annotation on line 99 acknowledges the risk. Requires further manual verification.
findings.remediation: Use os.Lstat to resolve symlink chains before allowing symlink creation in plugin archive extraction.

findings.description: CodeQL flags UnsafeClaimsWithoutVerification() usage in ext_jwt.go at line 346. However, this is inside the Test() probe function which only checks if a token is recognizable as an ext_jwt token before routing to the full auth pipeline. The actual authentication uses verified claims in authenticateAsService() and authenticateAsUserViaOBO(). This is a low-confidence finding.
findings.attack_vector: N/A - Test() is not an authentication decision point
findings.triage_note: Likely false positive. UnsafeClaimsWithoutVerification at line 346 is inside Test() which is a routing probe, not authentication. Verified against similar pattern in Phase 4 of prior audit (SAST-011 dropped as FP). Flag for Phase 5 triage.
findings.remediation: No immediate action required. Annotate code with comment explaining Test() purpose to prevent future false positive classifications.

findings.description: CodeQL SQL injection finding in xorm ORM internal layer (session_raw.go). The sqlStr parameter flows from user input through multiple layers. However, xorm uses parameterized queries (args...) for user-controlled values in normal usage. These findings likely represent the ORM's internal raw SQL execution path where callers are responsible for parameterization.
findings.attack_vector: SQL injection through xorm raw query execution
preconditions: Caller passes unsanitized user input as sqlStr (not args)
findings.triage_note: Requires per-call-site validation. xorm session_raw.go is ORM plumbing; actual risk depends on callers. High volume (8 SQL injection findings) suggests ORM internals are being flagged generically. Phase 5 should validate individual call sites.
findings.remediation: Audit all callers of session.Exec/Query that construct rawSQL strings. Ensure user input flows through args parameters, not sqlStr.

findings.description: CodeQL SQL injection: dashboard SQL query at line 151 includes user-provided value in query construction. This is in the legacy SQL dashboard path used by the K8s API aggregation layer.
findings.attack_vector: K8s API dashboard query with user-controlled filter value
preconditions: Authenticated user with dashboard read access
findings.triage_note: Needs manual call-site inspection to confirm if parameterization is missing or CodeQL is tracking through ORM builder patterns.
findings.remediation: Verify parameterization at sql_dashboards.go:151. Use query builder args pattern, not string concatenation.

findings.description: CodeQL path injection: user-provided URL path flows into file system path in static file handler at static.go:143 and :177. The static file handler serves plugin assets and frontend resources.
findings.attack_vector: Crafted URL with path traversal sequences in static asset request
preconditions: Access to static file serving endpoint
findings.triage_note: Needs verification - Go's http.ServeFile handles path cleanup, but path normalization differences may allow traversal in edge cases.
findings.remediation: Verify filepath.Clean and directory prefix check are applied to all user-controlled path segments in static.go before file I/O.

findings.description: CodeQL reflected XSS: user-provided value flows into response writer. response_writer.go:100 is a generic HTTP write wrapper; actual XSS depends on content-type headers set by callers. High false-positive rate for generic response writer sinks.
findings.attack_vector: User-controlled input reflected in HTTP response without encoding
preconditions: Caller does not set Content-Type: text/plain or application/json
findings.triage_note: Low confidence. response_writer.go is a middleware wrapper. Actual XSS risk depends on individual endpoint content-type handling. Flag for Phase 5 triage.
findings.remediation: Audit response_writer callers that write user-controlled content without explicit Content-Type: application/json or text/plain headers.

findings.description: Session token hashed with SHA256, which is fast and unsuitable for password hashing. However, this is for session token lookup (not password storage), where SHA256 is acceptable. CodeQL flags this as weak hashing for sensitive data.
findings.attack_vector: Offline brute-force of session token database dump using fast SHA256
preconditions: Access to session token database dump
findings.triage_note: Context-dependent. SHA256 for session token lookup is standard practice (tokens are random, not passwords). Low risk unless session tokens have insufficient entropy.
findings.remediation: If auth_token values have sufficient randomness (>=128 bits), SHA256 for lookup is acceptable. Verify token generation uses crypto/rand with adequate length.

findings.description: TLS configuration for renderer HTTP client does not explicitly set MinVersion. Go 1.22+ defaults to TLS 1.2 minimum, but explicit configuration is best practice for security-sensitive connections to the image renderer.
findings.attack_vector: TLS downgrade attack against renderer HTTP connection
preconditions: Custom CA cert configured for renderer (RendererCACert set)
findings.triage_note: Low risk in practice (Go 1.22+ defaults to TLS 1.2). Informational finding for defense-in-depth.
findings.remediation: Add MinVersion: tls.VersionTLS12 (or tls.VersionTLS13) to the TLS config at rendering.go:144.

findings.description: os.Create(filePath) called with nolint:gosec suppression in renderer HTTP mode. The filePath is the destination for renderer output. If filePath is influenced by attacker-controlled input, arbitrary file write is possible. This requires the filePath to originate from renderer response headers or body.
findings.attack_vector: Renderer response manipulates filePath destination via forged JWT
findings.triage_note: Medium confidence. nolint:gosec suppresses the file-write warning. Manual verification needed to confirm filePath source and whether it can be renderer-controlled.
findings.remediation: Verify filePath at http_mode.go:191 is generated server-side (e.g., os.CreateTemp) and not from renderer response body or headers.

findings.description: SQL UPDATE cloud_migration_snapshot SET status=? WHERE session_uid=? AND uid=? lacks org_id constraint. This is the execution path for CancelSnapshot (SAST-005). Multiple UPDATE statements with the same pattern exist in the function.
findings.attack_vector: Cancel snapshot in another org by knowing session_uid and snapshot_uid
findings.triage_note: Root cause of SAST-005. Confirmed at code level.
findings.remediation: Add AND org_id=? to all UPDATE WHERE clauses in UpdateSnapshot that operate on org-scoped resources.

findings.description: dangerouslySetInnerHTML renders plugin.details.readme without HTML sanitization. The readme content comes from the Grafana plugin catalog API (gnet). If the plugin catalog API is compromised or a malicious plugin is uploaded, the readme HTML could contain XSS payload. 13 of 19 dangerouslySetInnerHTML usages lack DOMPurify sanitization.
findings.attack_vector: Plugin with XSS payload in readme field served via plugin catalog
preconditions: Malicious plugin in grafana.com plugin catalog
findings.triage_note: Medium confidence. Plugin catalog is Grafana-controlled (supply chain assumption). However, defense-in-depth requires sanitization at rendering point.
findings.remediation: Apply DOMPurify.sanitize() or renderMarkdown() (which uses sanitizeTextPanelContent) to plugin.details.readme before rendering.

findings.description: dangerouslySetInnerHTML renders renderMarkdown(content) output for GenAI suggestions. renderMarkdown() uses sanitizeTextPanelContent() (xss library) by default, so this is sanitized. However, the noSanitize option could be passed to bypass sanitization. The content originates from a GenAI LLM response which could be manipulated via prompt injection.
findings.attack_vector: Prompt injection in GenAI suggestions causing XSS payload in rendered markdown
findings.triage_note: Low-medium. renderMarkdown sanitizes by default. Risk is theoretical prompt injection -> XSS chain. noSanitize option not used in this call.
findings.remediation: Confirm renderMarkdown is called without noSanitize:true option. Add explicit DOMPurify call as defense-in-depth.

findings.description: CodeQL bad-redirect-check: URL redirect uses a leading slash check but not a full origin check. This can lead to open redirect if the slash check is bypassable (e.g., //attacker.com is a valid protocol-relative URL with a leading slash).
findings.attack_vector: //attacker.com as redirect target passes leading slash check
preconditions: Application redirects user to URL from query parameter
findings.triage_note: Needs manual verification that static.go:164 sanitizes protocol-relative URLs. Prior open redirect CVEs in Grafana suggest this class of bugs has existed.
findings.remediation: Use url.Parse and check that the scheme is empty (relative URL) or matches the application origin, not just HasPrefix('/').

findings.description: External session token hashed with SHA256. Same analysis as SAST-014 - acceptable for token lookup if tokens have sufficient entropy.
findings.attack_vector: Offline brute-force of external session token
findings.triage_note: Low risk if token entropy is sufficient. Informational.
findings.remediation: Verify external session tokens use crypto/rand with >= 128 bits of entropy.

findings.description: OAuth token or credential material hashed with SHA256. Context determines risk (token lookup vs password hashing).
findings.attack_vector: Offline brute-force
findings.triage_note: Informational. Needs per-usage context to determine severity.
findings.remediation: If hashing OAuth access tokens for lookup, SHA256 is acceptable. If hashing passwords, upgrade to bcrypt/argon2.

findings.description: Reflected XSS in custom route metrics handler. Likely false positive - metrics endpoints typically return application/json or text/plain.
findings.attack_vector: User-controlled value reflected in metrics response without encoding
findings.triage_note: Low confidence. Metrics handlers should always return non-HTML content types. Likely FP.
findings.remediation: Verify Content-Type header is set to non-HTML type for all metrics endpoint responses.

coverage_notes: CodeQL analyzed 3672/5771 Go files (63.6%). Non-analyzed files are primarily generated code, test files, and files excluded by build tags.
Semgrep Pro engine produced 0 findings on targeted paths for golang/security-audit/trailofbits rulesets, reflecting mature security posture for common patterns in audited subsystems.

GitHub Actions scan: 89 workflows scanned, 0 findings. pull_request_target workflows (pr-commands.yml, pr-checks.yml) were reviewed manually - both check out the grafana-github-actions repo at main, not the PR branch code. Low risk.

The 283 go/log-injection and 38 go/clear-text-logging CodeQL findings are bulk ORM internal logging patterns in pkg/util/xorm/; these are not security findings in the traditional sense as the ORM logs SQL queries for debugging.
Semgrep Pro fallback: not required; Pro engine enabled successfully for all scans.
